verbatim_text: It's trained using distillation from much bigger models. And when you get it to solve these theorems, it produces a very, very long trace of tokens where it frequently switches directions. Instead of trying down a path and verifying that this proof attempt is clearly incorrect, let's move on to the next one. Instead of that, it tries one proof attempt, half-heartedly tries it, and tries to switch. The tokens are so random and the entropy is so high. Distillation is one of the reasons, because it's easy to minimize the distillation loss by mimicking this behavior. But it's harder to learn when to switch strategies and those kinds of things. RL is one way that you can go around. Roughly the idea is if the data that you're cloning is very heterogeneous, then that can make imitation terrible. But in RL, you can circumvent that because you're trying to have this mode-seeking behavior, like you're optimizing A reward, not trying to clone all the data. OK, I think we're out of time.
